coverage_notes: Single-threaded chat simulating a user incrementally building a small corridor showcase: orientation, naming, blocking, lighting, materials, interaction, polish, and review. Only ask/agent turns; no plan mode. Wording avoids naming automation tools or APIs—natural user goals only. Designed for analyzing prompt/context assembly and continuity across many sequential turns.

turns.request: Before we change anything in the project, give me two sentences on what kind of game or visualization this feels like from what you can tell so far.
We’re going to treat this chunk of work as a milestone called Corridor Pass One—use that phrase when you refer to this slice so our notes stay consistent.
Does Corridor Pass One match what’s already in the level, or are we steering into something new compared to the default setup?

Sketch a simple folder layout in words: one area for rough blockout meshes, one for materials we iterate on, and one for anything gameplay-related, even if folders are empty at first—then create that structure only if the editor already lets you do that safely.
In one short paragraph, what top-level names did you land on for those three buckets?
Block out a straight corridor segment with a floor, two walls, and a ceiling—keep scale human-sized, and leave one side open where a door or window might go later.
Roughly how wide and how tall does the corridor feel compared to a doorway you’d expect in real life—over, under, or about right?
Add a single dominant light so the corridor isn’t completely dark—bias it slightly warm or slightly cool, pick one mood and stick to it for this pass.
Describe the mood in three adjectives after that lighting pass, no technical jargon.
Put something underfoot that reads as worn concrete or old tile—not pristine—and adjust until the floor feels grounded in the space.
Does the floor feel like it belongs with the lighting mood, or is there a clash we should fix later?
Give the walls a different treatment from the floor so depth reads clearly at a glance, still keeping everything cheap and prototype-friendly.
If you had to explain the wall versus floor read to a non-artist, what would you say?
Place a simple prop or mesh partway down the corridor—something that could be a bench, crate, or barrier—just to break up the sightline.
What story does that prop suggest, even if we change it later—maintenance, abandonment, or something else?
Add a second, smaller light near the prop so it gets a little separation from the wall, without turning the whole scene into a spotlight carnival.
Where is your eye drawn first when you look down the corridor now—floor, far wall, or the prop—and why do you think that is?
Tighten the camera start so a first-time player isn’t staring at a blank wall—position the default view so the corridor invites you forward.
Does the invitation forward feel intentional, or accidental, in one sentence?

Run through the level in the normal preview way and tell me how many seconds it takes to walk from one end of the blocked corridor to the other at a calm pace—if something prevents that, say exactly what.
Was that walk length closer to five seconds, fifteen seconds, or something else entirely?
Identify anything that clips through the floor or floats obviously above it in that walk path and fix the worst offender if you can.
After that cleanup, is there still one nagging visual issue you’d queue for a second pass?
Duplicate the corridor module mentally into a short checklist: floor, walls, ceiling, main light, accent light, prop—confirm each exists in the scene hierarchy in plain language.
Which item on that checklist felt flimsiest when you verified it?
Introduce a placeholder sound idea only in text—where would a drip, hum, or distant door slam go—then we’ll decide if we add real audio later.
Which of those three sound ideas fits Corridor Pass One best, and why?
Pick a single color accent that appears somewhere small—trim, emissive strip, or detail—and make sure it doesn’t fight the warm or cool bias you chose earlier.
Name the accent color like you would in a design meeting, not like a shader parameter.
Add a readable label or text panel in the world that says Corridor Pass One somewhere appropriate, sized so it reads in the viewport without dominating the shot.
Does that label help orientation or feel like clutter?
Prepare for a later interaction: leave a clear spot near the prop where a button or note could sit—don’t build the interaction yet, just clear space and alignment.
If a player approached that spot, what would you want them to try first—look, listen, or touch—and why?
Sweep the corridor for harsh shadow acne or completely black pockets; soften the worst spot using whatever the project already exposes for lighting quality without exploding build times.
Did that pass change the mood from intimate to clinical, or stay in the same ballpark?
Capture a still frame that could go into a pitch deck—center the corridor, show depth, keep exposure sane—and save it under the project’s usual output area with a sensible filename.
In one line, what would you caption that image for stakeholders?
Write a three-bullet internal note: goal of Corridor Pass One, main risk, and next milestone name—keep it under fifty words total.
Which of those three bullets would you want a producer to remember after a five-minute meeting?
Revisit the milestone name Corridor Pass One and check that every major element we added still supports that title—rename the label in-world if the scene drifted.
If we renamed the milestone, what would be your second choice and why—one sentence?
Do a consistency pass: wall thickness, ceiling height, and prop scale should feel like one person built them—adjust the outlier that breaks the illusion most.
Which element still feels like a visitor from another project after that pass?
Add a subtle reason to return down the corridor—something you notice on the way back that you didn’t see heading in, even if it’s just composition or a faint glow.
Describe that return-path detail to someone who hasn’t seen the editor.
Summarize the project settings that matter for this demo—target frame of mind, not marketing—based on what’s in the ordinary project configuration files you can read.
Did anything in those settings surprise you for a corridor-sized slice?
Search the code or script side of the project for anything referencing the game module or default pawn—report what you find in everyday language, or say you found nothing.
If we later add interaction code, does the current module layout feel like a healthy place to grow, or cramped?
Clean up transient clutter from this session—extra actors spawned for testing, stray debug draws, duplicate lights—only what you’re confident is safe to remove.
What’s the single biggest thing you’d protect in the scene before handing it to another developer overnight?
Freeze a verbal snapshot: camera position, what’s selected, and what level or map context is active—so tomorrow’s session can pick up without guessing.
If tomorrow’s session forgot everything except your snapshot paragraph, what would still be ambiguous?
Close the loop on Corridor Pass One with a short retrospective: what worked, what we punted, and what we should not carry into the next milestone.
Rate your confidence that Corridor Pass One tells a coherent story from entry to exit on a scale of one to five and justify in one sentence.
Leave the editor in a tidy state for the next person: no modal dialogs left open, no half-finished renames obvious in the outliner, and a one-line note in your reply about what they’ll see first.
Finally, compress this whole thread into five bullet talking points I can paste into a standup—no tool names, just outcomes.
